seo.title: Fintech Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in fintech. Covers SOX, PCI DSS, GDPR.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for code generation in fintech environments introduces compounded risk: a single flawed output can propagate vulnerabilities into payment processing pipelines, trading systems, or customer data handlers with immediate regulatory and financial consequences. This checklist addresses the intersection of SOX, PCI DSS, GDPR, and SEC requirements with the unique attack surface of LLM-driven code synthesis, sandbox execution, and iterative refinement loops. Use it as a pre-deployment gate and ongoing operational standard for any AI coding agent touching financial infrastructure.

sections.heading: Code Safety & Static Analysis Controls
sections.description: Ensure every code artifact produced by the LLM agent is validated for security vulnerabilities, compliance violations, and dangerous patterns before it reaches any execution environment or code repository.

items.label: Mandatory SAST scan on all LLM-generated code
items.description: Run a Static Application Security Testing tool (e.g., Semgrep, Checkmarx, or Snyk Code) on every code snippet generated by the agent before sandbox execution or merge. Flag and block outputs containing SQL injection, buffer overflows, or insecure cryptographic primitives.

items.label: Hardcoded credential and secret detection
items.description: Integrate a secrets-scanning tool (e.g., TruffleHog, GitGuardian, or detect-secrets) into the agent output pipeline to reject any generated code containing API keys, database passwords, private keys, or PAN data patterns matching PCI DSS card number formats.

items.label: Cryptographic standard enforcement
items.description: Validate that all generated encryption code uses FIPS 140-2 compliant algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+, TLS 1.2+). Automatically reject outputs using deprecated algorithms such as MD5, SHA-1, DES, or RC4, which would violate PCI DSS Requirement 4 for data in transit.

items.label: PCI DSS scope boundary checks
items.description: Before executing generated code, verify it does not introduce new network paths or data flows that would expand the PCI DSS cardholder data environment (CDE) scope. Any code touching payment card data must be reviewed by a QSA-approved process.

items.label: SOX change management tagging
items.description: Tag all AI-generated code that modifies financial reporting logic, general ledger integrations, or audit trail systems as SOX-relevant. Require dual human approval before merging, consistent with SOX Section 302/404 internal control requirements.

items.label: Output license and IP compliance scan
items.description: Run generated code through a license compatibility checker (e.g., FOSSA or Black Duck) to ensure no GPL-contaminated or proprietary-licensed code is introduced into production fintech systems, which could create legal liability.

items.label: Explainability annotation for credit-related logic
items.description: When the agent generates code implementing credit scoring, loan decisioning, or risk models, require the agent to produce inline documentation explaining each decision factor. This satisfies ECOA/FCRA adverse action notice requirements and SEC model risk guidance.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Runtime Isolation
sections.description: The LLM agent's sandbox execution environment must be hardened to prevent generated code from accessing production financial data, exfiltrating secrets, or causing unintended side effects during iterative refinement cycles.

items.label: Network-isolated sandbox with egress allowlist
items.description: Execute all LLM-generated code in a container or VM with no default internet egress. Maintain an explicit allowlist of permitted outbound endpoints. Block all connections to production databases, payment processors, or external APIs during sandbox runs.

items.label: Synthetic data-only policy for sandbox inputs
items.description: Prohibit the use of real customer PII, PAN data, or account numbers as test inputs in the sandbox. Enforce this with a data classification scanner at sandbox ingress. Use tokenized or synthetically generated financial datasets that mirror production schemas.

items.label: Resource and execution time limits
items.description: Enforce CPU, memory, and wall-clock time limits on sandbox executions (e.g., 30-second timeout, 512MB RAM cap) to prevent denial-of-service conditions from runaway generated code and to limit the blast radius of malicious or erroneous outputs.

items.label: Filesystem write restrictions in sandbox
items.description: Mount sandbox filesystems as read-only except for designated temp directories. Prevent generated code from writing to paths that could persist malicious payloads, modify configuration files, or create backdoors that survive container restarts.

items.label: Syscall filtering via seccomp or equivalent
items.description: Apply a seccomp profile or AppArmor policy to sandbox containers that blocks dangerous syscalls (e.g., ptrace, mount, setuid) that could be used by generated code to escape the sandbox or escalate privileges.

items.label: Sandbox execution audit logging
items.description: Log all sandbox execution events including code inputs, stdout/stderr outputs, resource usage, and exit codes to an immutable audit log. Retain logs for a minimum of 7 years to satisfy SOX Section 802 record retention requirements.

items.label: Iterative refinement loop circuit breaker
items.description: Implement a maximum iteration limit (e.g., 10 refinement cycles) for the agent's test-fix loop. If the agent cannot produce passing, safe code within the limit, escalate to a human engineer rather than continuing to generate potentially divergent outputs.

items.label: Post-execution sandbox teardown verification
items.description: After each sandbox run, verify complete teardown of the execution environment including ephemeral storage wipe, network namespace deletion, and process tree cleanup. Confirm no residual state persists between agent sessions.

sections.heading: Dependency & Supply Chain Security
sections.description: AI-generated code frequently introduces third-party dependencies that may carry vulnerabilities, malicious packages, or license violations. In fintech, a compromised dependency can expose payment data or undermine audit integrity.

items.label: Dependency allowlist enforcement
items.description: Maintain an approved package allowlist for each language ecosystem (PyPI, npm, Maven, etc.). Reject any LLM-generated code that imports packages not on the allowlist without a formal security review. This prevents typosquatting and malicious package injection.

items.label: Automated CVE scan on all introduced dependencies
items.description: Run dependency vulnerability scanning (e.g., Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, or GitHub Dependabot) against every new package the agent introduces. Block deployment of code with CVSS score ≥7.0 vulnerabilities in its dependency tree.

items.label: Package integrity verification via hash pinning
items.description: Require all dependencies introduced by the agent to be pinned to specific versions with cryptographic hash verification (e.g., pip hash-checking mode, npm lockfile integrity). Prevent version drift that could introduce vulnerabilities post-approval.

items.label: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generation
items.description: Automatically generate a CycloneDX or SPDX SBOM for every code artifact produced by the agent. Store SBOMs in the audit repository to support incident response, regulatory inquiries, and SEC cybersecurity disclosure requirements under the 2023 rules.

items.label: Private package registry enforcement
items.description: Configure the sandbox and CI/CD pipeline to resolve packages only from an internal, vetted package mirror. Disable direct resolution from public registries to prevent dependency confusion attacks targeting internal package names.

items.label: License compatibility gate for financial IP
items.description: Automatically flag any dependency with AGPL, GPL, or SSPL licenses introduced by the agent, as these may require open-sourcing proprietary fintech algorithms or trading logic. Require legal review before allowing such dependencies.

items.label: Transitive dependency depth limit
items.description: Set a maximum transitive dependency depth (e.g., 5 levels) for agent-generated code. Deep dependency trees increase supply chain attack surface and complicate PCI DSS Requirement 6.3 vulnerability management obligations.

sections.heading: Governance, Auditability & Human Oversight
sections.description: Regulatory frameworks governing fintech require that AI-generated code changes be traceable, explainable, and subject to human authorization. This section ensures the agent operates within a governed workflow that satisfies SOX, SEC, and BSA/AML audit requirements.

items.label: Human-in-the-loop approval gate for production deployments
items.description: Require explicit approval from a licensed engineer and a compliance officer before any AI-generated code is deployed to production environments handling customer funds, payment processing, or regulated reporting. Document approvals with timestamps and approver identity.

items.label: Immutable audit trail for all agent actions
items.description: Log every prompt submitted to the LLM agent, every code artifact generated, every sandbox execution result, and every human approval decision to a write-once, tamper-evident audit log (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock). Retain for 7 years per SOX requirements.

items.label: Model version and prompt template versioning
items.description: Record the exact LLM model version, system prompt, and temperature settings used for each code generation session. This enables reproducibility for regulatory investigations and supports SEC model risk management expectations for algorithmic systems.

items.label: BSA/AML pattern detection in generated code
items.description: Review agent-generated code that touches transaction monitoring, suspicious activity reporting (SAR), or customer due diligence (CDD) workflows against FinCEN guidance. Flag any logic that could suppress alerts, bypass thresholds, or omit required BSA reporting fields.

items.label: GDPR data minimization review for generated data handlers
items.description: When the agent generates code that processes EU customer data, validate that the code implements data minimization (Article 5), purpose limitation, and does not introduce new personal data fields without a documented lawful basis and DPIA update.

items.label: Incident response playbook for AI-generated code failures
items.description: Maintain a documented incident response playbook specific to AI-generated code failures in production. Include rollback procedures, regulatory notification timelines (e.g., 72-hour GDPR breach notification, SEC Form 8-K material cybersecurity incident disclosure), and customer communication templates.

items.label: Periodic red-team testing of agent outputs
items.description: Conduct quarterly adversarial testing of the code generation agent by attempting to elicit outputs that bypass security controls, generate financial fraud logic, or produce code that manipulates audit trails. Document findings and remediation actions.

items.label: Agent capability scope limitation policy
items.description: Define and enforce a written policy specifying which codebases, repositories, and system components the AI agent is permitted to generate code for. Explicitly prohibit the agent from generating code for core banking ledger systems, cryptographic key management, or regulatory reporting engines without elevated review.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 6.2.4 mandates that all bespoke and custom software is protected from common software attacks. AI-generated code must pass the same secure coding review process as human-written code, and the organization remains fully liable for vulnerabilities introduced by LLM outputs. Document your AI code generation process in your System Description submitted to your QSA.

SOX Section 404 requires management to assess the effectiveness of internal controls over financial reporting. Any AI agent that generates or modifies code in financial reporting systems must be included in your ICFR scope. The agent's approval workflows, audit logs, and change management controls must be documented and tested annually by your external auditor.

SEC Cybersecurity Disclosure Rules (effective December 2023) require public companies to disclose material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days on Form 8-K and to describe cybersecurity risk management processes in annual Form 10-K filings. A breach caused by AI-generated code introducing a vulnerability into payment systems would likely qualify as material and trigger these disclosure obligations.

BSA/AML regulations enforced by FinCEN require that transaction monitoring systems and SAR filing logic maintain integrity and cannot be circumvented. AI-generated code modifying AML detection thresholds, alert suppression logic, or customer risk scoring must undergo independent model validation consistent with OCC Model Risk Management guidance (SR 11-7) before deployment, and changes must be logged in the model inventory.

cta.heading: Automate Your Fintech Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously enforce this checklist across every AI code generation session, automatically blocking non-compliant outputs and generating audit-ready reports for your SOX, PCI DSS, and SEC obligations.
